agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Memory leak from never-cleaned-up WebSocket listeners and CourierTracker instances will OOM the service; unguarded JSON.parse will crash on malformed messages."
  },
  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "Memory leak: WebSocket listeners and CourierTracker instances never cleaned up",
      "risk": "Each courier reconnect (avg every 5 min) creates a new CourierTracker that registers listeners on the WebSocket. There is no 'close' handler to remove listeners and no teardown method. Old CourierTracker instances remain reachable via the WebSocket's listener list, preventing garbage collection. Over 3 months with N couriers reconnecting ~288 times/day each, this accumulates hundreds of thousands of orphaned objects — explaining the steadily increasing memory usage.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners()",
      "reproduction": "1. Create 100 CourierTracker instances with reconnecting WebSockets. 2. Disconnect and reconnect each 50 times. 3. Observe process.memoryUsage().heapUsed growing monotonically — old instances are never freed.",
      "fix": "Add a 'close' handler on the WebSocket that removes all listeners. Store listener references so they can be removed. Implement a destroy() method called on disconnect: `this.ws.on('close', () => { this.ws.removeAllListeners(); }); ` Also consider using a Map<courierId, CourierTracker> to ensure only one tracker exists per courier, destroying the old one on reconnect.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service stability — OOM will eventually kill the process"
    },
    {
      "title": "Unguarded JSON.parse will crash the process on malformed WebSocket data",
      "risk": "A single malformed message from any courier (network corruption, client bug, deliberate injection) throws a SyntaxError that is uncaught. In Node.js, an unhandled exception in a WebSocket 'message' listener will crash the entire process, taking down tracking for ALL couriers — not just the one that sent bad data.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners() — `const location = JSON.parse(data);`",
      "reproduction": "Send a non-JSON string (e.g., empty string, truncated payload, or binary frame) over the WebSocket. The process throws `SyntaxError: Unexpected token` and crashes.",
      "fix": "Wrap in try/catch: `try { const location = JSON.parse(data); if (typeof location?.lat !== 'number' || typeof location?.lng !== 'number') return; this.updateLocation(location); } catch (e) { console.error(`Invalid message from courier ${this.courierId}:`, e); }`",
      "time_to_fix": "10 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service availability — single bad message crashes entire service"
    },
    {
      "title": "Fire-and-forget database write with no error handling or backpressure",
      "risk": "updateLocation calls db.couriers.updateLocation without awaiting the promise or catching errors. If the DB is slow or down: (1) errors are silently swallowed — location data is lost with no indication, (2) promises pile up unbounded since there is no backpressure, compounding the memory leak, (3) a DB connection pool can be exhausted by queued writes from hundreds of couriers sending rapid updates.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "CourierTracker.updateLocation()",
      "reproduction": "Simulate a slow database (add 2s latency). Send location updates every 100ms from 50 couriers. Observe unresolved promise count growing unbounded and eventual connection pool exhaustion.",
      "fix": "Await the DB call and handle errors. Add throttling/debouncing so rapid GPS pings don't flood the DB: `private async updateLocation(location: { lat: number; lng: number }): Promise<void> { try { await db.couriers.updateLocation(this.courierId, location.lat, location.lng); } catch (e) { console.error(`DB write failed for ${this.courierId}:`, e); } }`",
      "time_to_fix": "20 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Data integrity — location updates silently lost during DB issues"
    }
  ],
  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "No input validation on lat/lng values",
      "description": "location.lat and location.lng are used directly without range validation. Invalid coordinates (lat > 90, lng > 180, NaN, Infinity) will be written to the database, corrupting courier location data and potentially breaking downstream consumers like ETA calculations or map rendering.",
      "location": "CourierTracker.updateLocation()"
    },
    {
      "title": "Console.error is insufficient for production observability",
      "description": "Errors are logged to console.error with no structured logging, no metrics emission, and no alerting integration. In production, courier disconnects and errors should emit metrics (e.g., courier.error counter, courier.active gauge) so ops can detect systemic issues before they become outages.",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners() — error handler"
    }
  ],
  "suggestions": [
    "Throttle or debounce location updates to reduce DB write volume — GPS pings every 1-2 seconds are common but writing every one is wasteful; batch or sample at 10-15 second intervals",
    "Add a connection registry (Map<string, CourierTracker>) to enforce one-tracker-per-courier and enable graceful shutdown of all connections",
    "Consider using ws.once('close', ...) or AbortController patterns for cleaner lifecycle management"
  ],
  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "This code is actively leaking memory in production. The fix (adding cleanup handlers) is safe to deploy and should be expedited.",
    "rollback_capability": "Fix is additive (adding listeners and try/catch), safe to roll back if needed.",
    "backward_compatibility": "No external API changes — purely internal lifecycle fix.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "None. No metrics on active connections, memory growth, or DB write failures. Must add before deploying fix to verify it resolves the leak.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": false,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": []
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Revert the commit. The memory leak will resume but service is currently surviving it."
  },
  "escalations": [],
  "confidence": {
    "level": 95,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Memory leak from missing WebSocket close/cleanup handlers — textbook Node.js leak pattern",
      "JSON.parse crash — deterministic failure on any non-JSON input",
      "Fire-and-forget DB writes — observable via promise accumulation under load"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact memory growth rate depends on unknown factors: number of couriers, reconnect frequency, payload sizes"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "db.couriers.updateLocation returns a Promise (standard for Node.js DB drivers)",
      "No external connection manager exists that handles cleanup outside this class",
      "WebSocket is from the 'ws' library or compatible API"
    ]
  }
}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this code for production issues.

Key issues I can see immediately:

1. **Memory leak**: No cleanup of WebSocket listeners or CourierTracker instances. When couriers disconnect and reconnect, new instances are created but old ones are never cleaned up. The WebSocket listeners keep references alive, preventing GC.

2. **No JSON.parse error handling**: Malformed WebSocket messages will throw and crash the process.

3. **No await/error handling on db call**: The database update is fire-and-forget with no error handling.

Let me produce the output.
